summary:
The Battles of Tarain (1191 and 1192 AD) were two successive engagements fought near the town of Tarain (modern Taraori in Haryana, India) between the Ghurid sultan Muhammad of Ghor and the Rajput confederation led by [[event:3526|Prithviraj Chauhan]], king of the Chahamana dynasty, whose outcome decided the fate of northern India for centuries. At the First Battle of Tarain in 1191, Prithviraj's forces achieved a significant victory — Muhammad was wounded and nearly captured, and the Ghurid army withdrew — giving the Rajput coalition false confidence in their ability to halt the Afghan advance. Muhammad of Ghor returned to Afghanistan, reorganized his forces, and launched a methodical second invasion the following year.

The Second Battle of Tarain in 1192 was catastrophic for the Rajputs. Muhammad deployed a coordinated cavalry assault using the Ghurid army's superior horse-archery tactics: waves of mounted archers harassed the Rajput lines without committing to close combat, exhausting the slower Indian war elephants and infantry, before a final decisive charge broke the Rajput formation. Prithviraj was captured fleeing the battlefield and was subsequently executed. The battle was transformative in its consequences: within months, Ghurid forces seized Delhi, Ajmer, and the upper Gangetic plain. Muhammad's general Qutb ud-Din Aibak went on to found the [[event:2253|Delhi Sultanate]], establishing the first enduring Muslim political power in the Indian subcontinent's heartland. The Second Battle of Tarain is therefore considered one of the most consequential battles in South Asian history — a turning point after which the political and religious character of northern India was permanently altered.

read_more_links.title: Qutb ud-Din Aibak Delhi Sultanate founded
